coredata.dc:title: A two-photon mitochondria-targeted fluorescent probe for the detection of pH fluctuation in tumor and living cells
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Fei
Liu, Dandan
Shen, Yu
Liu, Jiejie
Li, Dandan
Tian, Xiaohe
Zhang, Qiong
Wu, Jieying
Li, Shengli
Tian, Yupeng
coredata.dc:description: Developing small-molecular fluorescent sensors for the detection of pH fluctuation is highly demanded yet challenging. Herein, a novel pH sensor, stilbazolium-based compound L1, was rational designed and developed. Notably, the two-photon activity responds strongly to protonation due to the increased extent of symmetrical charge transfer within molecule. Benefiting from the considerable two-photon activity and biocompatibility, it was observed that L1 could certainly sensor pH changes in living cells by using two-photon confocal microscopy and stimulated emission depletion nanoscopy methods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: pH sensor
Two-photon
Symmetrical charge transfer
Living tumors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism of probe L1 response to pH

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Molecular structures (PF6 −are omitted for clarity). (b) The crystal structures of L1 and L2. (PF6 − and all H atoms are omitted for clarity).
(a) The photographs of L1 at diverse pH values. Absorption (b) and fluorescence spectra (c) of L1 (c = 1.0 × 10−5 mol/L) in PBS with various pH values.
(a) Sigmoidal fitting of the pH-dependent emission. Inset: the good linearity in the pH range 3.86–5.86 of L1. (b) Time scan of fluorescence intensity of L1 in response to pH changes in water.
The two-photon absorption cross-sections spectra (a) and change of two-photon absorption cross-section plot (b) of L1 (at 900 nm) at various pH values.

At various pH values, 1H NMR spectra of L1 in D2O, Considering above, the “turn-off” behavior of one-photon fluorescence in acidic environment could be ascribed the variation of molecular stacking state [27]. Upon the protonation of the terminal hydroxyl group of L1, the hydrophilicity of the entire molecule increased. The resultant loosely packing aggregates promotes intramolecular rotation, which consume most of the energy and thus result in weak one-photon fluorescence (turn-off behavior). The related experiment was performed to confirm this mechanism (Fig.S14).

(a) Confocal fluorescence images of HeLa cells with L1 in different pH PBS buffers (λex = 900 nm, λem = 580–620 nm). Scale bar: 20 μm. (b) STED micrographs of HeLa cells incubated with L1. Scale bar: 100 nm. (c) Zoomed in STED micrograph from selected regions from (b) showing fibrillar mitochondria.
In vivo (a) One-photon (λex = 480 nm, λem = 580–620 nm), (b) Two-photon λex = 900 nm, λem = 580–620 nm) micrographs of L1 in the tumor-bearing mice model within 24 h.

head.ce:title: A two-photon mitochondria-targeted fluorescent probe for the detection of pH fluctuation in tumor and living cells

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing small-molecular fluorescent sensors for the detection of pH fluctuation is highly demanded yet challenging. Herein, a novel pH sensor, stilbazolium-based compound L1, was rational designed and developed. Notably, the two-photon activity responds strongly to protonation due to the increased extent of symmetrical charge transfer within molecule. Benefiting from the considerable two-photon activity and biocompatibility, it was observed that L1 could certainly sensor pH changes in living cells by using two-photon confocal microscopy and stimulated emission depletion nanoscopy methods.

ce:section.ce:para: Intracellular pH plays prominent roles in regulating and maintaining multiple biological processes, such as cell adhesion, cell proliferation and enzyme activity [1–3]. Alterations of intracellular pH caused oxygen deprivation in the entire cells and cytometaplasia resulting in cancerous [4]. Thereinto, tumor cells exhibit the frequent occurrence of a more acidic pH as compared to the normal cells, and this physiological characteristic has been successfully applied in pH-responsive and tumor-targeting drug delivery [5–8]. Therefore, precisely monitoring the fluctuation of the intracellular pH offers a useful tool for understanding the cellular functions and tumor processes. More importantly, mitochondria are important organelles for maintaining the cellular homeostasis, including the main source of ‘‘energy factories’’ through oxidative phosphorylation to produce adenosine triphosphate (ATP) [9–11]. As such, fluorescence imaging of mitochondrial could offer a better understanding of diseases and drug development. Particularly, the development of a mitochondrial probe for monitoring the dynamic pH in living cells is of great interest to understand various diseases at the cellular level.

To meet this grand challenge, various strategies have been developed to monitor the pH fluctuation, such as magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS), chemical exchange saturation transfer (CEST) and microelectrode [12]. However, these methods suffered from difficult operation and low resolution, as well as maximized cell damage. Fortunately, fluorescent probes have been developed with simple operation and fast response, which is a promising approach to monitor pH changes in living cells [13–17]. Many of them are one-photon pH sensing probes, which gave rise to low tissue penetration and photon-damage. Two-photon-active materials, utilizing two near-infrared (NIR) photons as the excitation source, offer several advantages, including deeper tissue penetration, higher spacial resolution and weaker specimen photodamage [18–20]. Bearing the above points in mind, two-photon fluorescent pH sensing probes could serve as judicious candidates.

Spurred on by this, a fluorescent pH probe L1, bearing two-photon activity was designed and synthesized relied on the following consideration: (1) L1 was constructed by electron-donating groups (aromatic amino) and electron-withdrawing moieties (pyridinium salt) with D-π-A model. (2) Benefiting from the cationic behavior of pyridinium salt, L1 can make it target the negatively charged mitochondria [21]. (3) The introduction of hydroxy groups in L1 not only increased the solubility but also could be protonated in acid solution, giving rise to sensitive response to pH, which differed from the traditional nitrogen-proton exchange family [22–24]. Meanwhile, a similar compound L2 was also designed to better understand the pH sensing mechanism. As expected, L1 shows reversible pH response and a good linear response from pH 7.00 to 4.50. The results show that, with the acidity increased, one-photon fluorescence of L1 exhibited quenching effect triggered by the change of molecular stacking state. Notably, the two-photon activity of L1 manifested obvious enhancement due to the increased extent of symmetrical charge transfer within molecule upon the increasing of acidity. Imaging of tumor-bearing mice indicates that L1 has potential clinical application value for tumor imaging and tumor diagnosis.

Synthesis of
L1
and
L2 . The corresponding aldehyde derivative (10 mmol) and M (pyridinium salt derivative, see supporting information) (2.35 g, 10 mmol) were separately dissolved in ethanol, and 8 drops piperidine were added dropwise. The solution was reacted for 12 h at 75 °C, changing from colorless to red. After cooling to room temperature, the precipitate was collected by filtration, washed by ethanol, and then removed solvent in vacuum, got red solids.

The crystal structures of L1 and L2 were shown in Fig. 1 . The crystallography data, selected bond lengths and angles were given in Table S1 and Table S2, respectively. Both L1 and L2 crystallized with monoclinic system with Pī space group. The dihedral angle between the benzene and pyridine ring was 4.16° in L1 and 4.47° in L2, revealing that L1 and L2 have coplanar structures. Besides, the bond lengths of C(10)-C(11) (1.46 Å), C(11) = C(12) (1.30 Å), C(12)-C(15) (1.49 Å) in compound L1, and C(4)-C(7) (1.44 Å), C(7) = C(8) (1.30 Å), C(8)-C(9) (1.49 Å) in compound L2 are all between single and double carbon-carbon bond. Those structural features manifest that L1 and L2 possess excellent coplanar and highly π-electron delocalized system, which favors two-photon activity [25].

L1 and L2 both displayed two absorption bands around 285 and 480 nm (Fig. S1). The low energy bands could be ascribed as the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) transition (HOMO→LUMO+2 in L1, HOMO→LUMO in L2), while the high energy bands might be assigned as π-π∗ transition of the whole molecule (corresponding to the HOMO →LUMO and HOMO-1→ LUMO, respectively), which was further confirmed by TD-DFT calculations [26]. Interestingly, the appearance colors of the solutions for L1 change from yellow to colorless with the acidity increased (Fig. 2 a), which implied that L1 is sensitive to pH change. To further investigate the pH sensitivity of the probe, UV-vis absorption spectra of L1 at different pH values were recorded in PBS buffer (Fig. 2). As shown in Fig. 2b, the absorption intensity centered around 450 nm of L1 decreased gradually with increasing the pH values, accompanied by a new absorption band at 340 nm emerged and increased. However, L2 displayed a negligible change in the position of absorption bands upon varying the pH values (Fig. S5).

Encouraged by the above results, we set out to investigate the variation of single-photon fluorescence upon pH fluctuation. As shown in Fig. 2c, when the pH values decreased from 7.01 to 3.57, the fluorescence intensity of L1 at 590 nm gradually decreased (“turn-off” effect), which is in accordance with the results of fluorescence lifetime measurement (Fig. S6), indicating that L1 is highly sensitive to pH changes. The non-linear fit of the emission intensity of L1 with the pH values changes using a sigmoid curve yielded a pKa value of 4.82 for compound L1 (Fig. 3 a), which matched well with a typical pH of tumor cells. It indicates that L1 can be applied for cell imaging in an acidic environment.

In order to confirm a fast response of L1 to pH change, the solution of L1 was adjusted to alkaline (pH = 13) or acidic (pH = 1) by the addition of NaOH or HCl. Upon the addition of HCl to a solution of L1, the fluorescence intensity at 590 nm was immediately changed within 14 s. Upon further additions of NaOH and HCl, the fluorescence emission intensity periodically changed. Such a change can be repeated for many cycles (5 cycles tested) and the fluorescence intensity is almost unchanged (Fig. 3b). As a result, it demonstrated that L1 can show a reversible fluorescence change to pH variation with a fast response time and high stability.

The two-photon excited fluorescence (2PEF) spectra of L1 and L2 in DMSO with 1 × 10−3 mol/L were recorded at its maximum excitation wavelength with a pulse duration of 140 fs at 500 mW. As shown in Fig. S6, the maximum 2 PA cross-section (σmax) for L1 is approximately 201 GM at 900 nm, which is larger than L2 (λex = 880 nm, δmax = 146 GM) due to its well planar molecular structure. To further explore the potential of L1 in 2PFM pH-response applications, the 2PEF properties of L1 at various pH values were investigated in the PBS medium. Upon the addition of HCl, the 2PEF intensity was obvious increased (“turn-on” effect). Meanwhile, the σmax values at 900 nm of L1 increased from 91 GM to 390 GM by decreasing the pH values from 7.0 to 3.8 (Fig. 4 ). All of these results have strongly suggested that L1 may be an excellent pH-dependent reversible probe which possessing a larger σ value in the near-infrared wavelength, providing a potential clinical application in the live cells and tissues imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of probe L1 response to pH
ce:section.ce:para: The “turn-off” and “turn-on” behavior of one- and two-photon fluorescence for L1 in acidic environment motivated us to investigate the pH sensing mechanism. The protonation of hydroxy groups within L1 may make sense. To confirm the above, 1H NMR and ESI-mass spectra of L1 at different pH values were carried out. After the addition of HCl, the chemical displacement values of benzene ring protons and ethylene matrix substrates did not change significantly. However, the hydrogen from hydroxyl groups of L1 (H1, H1’) had an obvious down-field shift (Fig. 5 ). Furthermore, a new molecular ion peak at 343.2108 (L1+2H+) was observed, which further confirmed the protonation of the hydroxyl moiety. However, the above experimental phenomena were not observed in L2 without the hydroxyl groups (Figs. S10 and S12), which further proved the pH mechanism of L1.

Additionally, the two-photon “turn-on” effect of L1 in acidic environment should stem from the increased extent of symmetrical charge transfer within molecule [28]. Upon the protonation of the terminal hydroxyl group of L1, the electron-donating capacity of the aromatic amino group is canceled (actually reversed into electron-accepting), which results in the enhanced transition dipole moment and two-photon activity.

To evaluate the toxicity of L1, an MTT assay was performed in HeLa cell lines (Fig. S15). L1 shows low toxicity at different concentrations due to the exceptionally high biocompatibility. In addition, L1 exhibited well photo-stability (Fig. S16). It motivated us to investigate the confocal fluorescence imaging of HeLa cells incubated with L1 (Fig. S17). Interestingly, the L1 specifically target to the fibrillar-shaped region of the cytosol, which might be the mitochondria. To confirm the cellular location of L1, co-localization experiments were conducted with Mito-tracker Red, a fluorescent organelle markers targeted to mitochondria. As shown in Fig. S17, the Pearson correlation factors (Rr) strongly suggested that L1 targeted the intracellular mitochondria, since the cationic nature might easily target the high membrane potential. Simultaneously, the Rr-values clearly showed that the high overlap for L1, indicated that the fluorescence of L1 completely overlapped with mitochondria due to the cationic lipophilic nature, which verified our design idea and indicated L1 exhibited a relatively higher efficiency with mitochondrial staining.

To evaluate the change of pH in living cell, HeLa cells incubated with L1 and pretreated with acid/alkali solution (pH 4.5, 7.0 and 9.0), and then imaged under two-photon confocal microscopy. Fig. 6 a showed that under laser irradiation, an attenuated green luminescence of mitochondria was observed in HeLa cells at pH 9.0. When the pH value increased to 4.5, the green fluorescence intensity in mitochondria was significantly enhanced. These results demonstrate that L1 can monitor changes in cell pH in real time and can be used as an two-photon intracellular pH probe, which has great significance in biology. Additionally, stimulated emission depletion (STED) nanoscopy of L1 was performed owing to the longer excitation wavelength and good photo-stability. As shown in Fig. 6b, the ultradetail of a single mitochondrion revealed fibrillar structures, highly correspondent to the L1 that was distributed within the mitochondrial.

Taking advantage of STED super-resolution micrographs of probe L1, the linear response of pH value in living cells is of great significant for detecting the change of pH in tumor cells more precisely. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the strong signal of L1 in the MCF-7 tumor-bearing mice was observed at the tumor after siting 1 h post-injection, nonetheless, the tumor-bearing mouse model (pH = 4.5) injected with L1 shown distinct tumor-targeting capability. Moreover, the enhanced emission was observed in the tumor-bearing mouse by two-photon excitation (Fig. 7b). It demonstrated that L1 is an excellent example for monitoring tumor-targeting capability.

In summary, a novel pH-dependent NIR fluorescence probe has been designed and synthesized. Thanks to the introduction of hydroxyl groups, “turn-off” and “turn-on” behavior of one- and two-photon fluorescence for L1 in acidic environment could be observed. The pH sensing mechanism was investigated by 1H NMR and ESI-mass spectra, which was further analyzed by molecule stacking and symmetrical charge transfer paths. 2PM images revealed that L1 mainly localized to the mitochondria of HeLa cells, and certainly monitor pH changes in living cells. Moreover, STED super-resolution micrographs were clearly visualized the fibrillar structures of mitochondrial. Furthermore, L1 was also successfully applied to tumor targeted imaging due to its excellent water solubility and long excitation/emission wavelengths.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51672002, 51432001, 21701160, 21602003, 21871003 and 51772002), Anhui Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (1708085MC68), and Anhui University Doctor Startup Fund (S01002127), and Returnees innovation and entrepreneurship key support program, the Higher Education Revitalization Plan Talent Project (2013).
